Title: Navigating the rise of finfluencers: a multidimensional quality framework for strategic partnerships
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1057/s41264-025-00334-7
Time Published: 2026-03-04T16:15:37Z
Full Content:
In the film Wall Street: Money Never Sleeps (2010), the antihero Gordon Gekko reflects on the moral ambiguity of modern financial services, stating: “(.) the beauty of the deal [taking other people’s money]: no one is responsible”. This phrase is gaining new relevance as financial influencers (finfluencers) are increasingly filling the financial advice gap by operating on various social platforms within a legal gray zone and with limited accountability (Barrett 2022; Moss et al. 2022; Pflücke 2022, 2023). Even though there is no common definition yet, we follow Mölders et al. (2025), who define finfluencers as “personal brands who build relationships with an engaged audience through a regular flow of consistent, authentic and distinctive content posted on at least one social media platform that relates to, but is not limited to, personal finance and investment tips.”Footnote 1 While finfluencers can be seen as a distinct subset of expert-oriented and utilitarian influencers, their approaches diverge significantly: some position themselves as expert voices, drawing on formal qualifications, licenses, or professional experience to establish authority, while others adopt a utilitarian role, providing simplified, accessible financial tips for lay audiences (Mölders et al. 2025).Footnote 2 This dual positioning highlights the tension between perceived expertise and actual credentials, as well as the differing levels of accountability tied to each role. Comparable distinctions have been noted in the wider influencer marketing literature (e.g., Campbell and Farrell 2020; Vrontis et al. 2021; Han and Balabanis 2024) and are similarly relevant for other regulated domains, including medicine and politics (e.g., Ding et al. 2023). The rise of finfluencers was fueled by a number of factors, including the continuous expansion of global internet and social media usage (We Are Social et al. 2025), the pandemic (Boyde 2023; Davies 2023; Fava 2023), the growing valuation of global influencer marketing exceeding $30 billion in 2025 (Statista 2025), and the rising demand for financial literacy and advice (Calcagno and Monticone 2015; Barrett 2022). While the investment volume driven by finfluencers is difficult to pinpoint, finfluencers are currently outperforming their peers in other influencer niches, with double the follower growth on platforms like TikTok and Instagram (Emplifi 2024). Indeed, they seem to have a significant impact on the financial decision-making of younger generations as recent surveys show: e.g., in the United Kingdom (UK), over 44% of Gen Z state using information from social media for investment decisions; in the United States (US), 37% of Gen Z say finfluencers play a major role in their investment decisions (FINRA Foundation and CFA Institute 2023); and in Germany, more than half of Gen Z and Millennials view social media as an alternative to traditional financial advice (German Federal Financial Supervisory Authority [BaFin] 2024). However, finfluencers tend to concentrate on high-risk asset classes such as cryptocurrencies, where social media hype and fear of missing out (FOMO) often drive investment decisions (Yamamoto et al. 2020; FINRA Foundation and CFA Institute 2023; Merkley et al. 2024). A similar phenomenon has been observed in NFTs (non-fungible tokens), where pricing and trading volumes are closely tied to finfluencer activity rather than fundamentals (e.g., Kapoor et al. 2022; Horky et al. 2023). Thus, finfluencers can also be seen as facilitators of speculative retail investing (Mölders et al. 2025) and the crypto ecosystem. This is consistent with headlines such as “Eight Twitter finance gurus charged with criminal pump-and-dump scheme” (Robertsen 2022), which highlight the financial risks associated with “finfluencing charlatans” (adapted from Berk and Binsbergen 2022). In this prominent case, also known as Atlas Trading, eight Twitter-based finfluencers were indicted by US authorities for stock manipulation and defrauding retail investors of over $100 million (Securities and Exchange Commission [SEC] 2022b; US Department of Justice 2022). Beyond this, there have been numerous cases of fraudulent promotion of financial services (also by celebrities) and “get rich quick” investment schemes via social media (e.g., Lukpat 2022; Michaels 2022; Davies 2023). In response to such significant financial losses and consumer harm, regulators and authorities around the globe are turning their eyes on finfluencers (e.g., Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets [AFM] 2021a); Australian Securities and Investments Commission 2022; BaFin 2022; SEC 2022a, 2022b; European Securities and Markets Authority 2023; Financial Conduct Authority 2023a, 2023b, 2023c; Securities and Exchange Board of India 2023). For instance, the German regulatory body has issued the following warning: “On social media, you will find real experts – but many self-proclaimed experts as well. Both types are also to be found among the financial influencers (known as ‘finfluencers’)” (BaFin 2022). It is therefore not surprising that current research predominately discusses consumer harm and protection and corresponding legal perspectives (e.g., Miller 2022; Pflücke 2022, 2023; Hall 2023; Khurana 2023; Ramaswamy 2023; Stefanou 2023; Guan 2023, 2024). Apart from the consumer protection perspective, finfluencers could also be integrated as strategic business partners (Enke and Borchers 2019; Borchers and Enke 2021). This is especially relevant for financial services marketing (e.g., Mensah and Amenuvor 2022; Ho and Amin 2023; Nguyen et al. 2024). However, businesses seeking strategic partnerships with finfluencers are at risk of damaging their own reputation by potentially collaborating with finfluencing charlatans (e.g., Campbell and Farrell 2020; McMullan 2023). In consequence, we perceive a gap in the current academic discourse regarding the quality of finfluencers, which has been addressed only one-dimensionally based on their investing performance (Kakhbod et al. 2023; Espeute and Preece 2024). In response, we address this gap and follow calls for further research on new technologies, especially social media and finfluencers (Bhaskar et al. 2023; Hudders and Lou 2023; Bapat and Vashisht 2024; Yeo et al. 2024) and provide a complementary solution to economically costly over-regulation (Berk and Binsbergen 2022). Thus, our study aims to derive a multidimensional finfluencer quality (FinQ) framework to guide finfluencers’ stakeholders to select high-quality strategic business partners. Hence, we formulate our guiding research question (RQ) as follows: RQ What are quality indicators of trustworthy finfluencers and how can these indicators be systematically integrated into a multidimensional quality framework for facilitating strategic partnerships? We applied a mixed-method approach to answer the RQ. In the first step, we conceptualized a preliminary FinQ framework based on existing theory. In a second step, we used 12 expert interviews to refine and complement the preliminary FinQ framework, which resulted in the identification of 40 quality indicators (N = 40). These quality indicators can be categorized into five quality dimensions (QDs): (QD1) Compliance, (QD2) Content and Communication, (QD3) Credibility, (QD4) Profile Structure and (QD5) Engagement. In a last step, the refined FinQ framework was validated in terms of practical applicability and operationalization by a subsequent multiple-case study selecting five German-speaking finfluencers on Instagram. While our study tackles quality uncertainty surrounding finfluencers, it also encourages businesses to form strategic partnerships with credible, high-quality finfluencers, particularly in financial services marketing. The FinQ framework supports the professionalization of such partnerships by enabling more informed selection decisions. While it cannot fully eliminate charlatans, it helps financial service providers identify trustworthy partners, avoid reputational risks, and raise standards over time. Beyond financial services, the framework can also inform regulatory efforts, consumer protection, and strategic collaborations in other areas, such as listed firms’ financial communication or governmental financial literacy campaigns. The business model of finfluencers involves referral and reward networks potentially leading to conflicts of interest (Campbell and Farrell 2020; Reingen and Kernan 1986; Verlegh et al. 2013). Such networks usually compensate finfluencers on a commission basis upon referral of a client for a specific financial service or product (e.g., by using affiliate links or promo codes) (AFM 2021b). Thus, we assume that finfluencers face moral hazard because of their conflicted business model (Arrow 1985; Alon et al. 2021). In this context, when the lemon principle, referring to the problem of quality uncertainty in markets with information asymmetry between buyers and sellers (Akerlof 1970), is applied to finfluencers (sellers), their stakeholders (buyers; here consumers or followers and businesses seeking partnerships) face quality uncertainty, wherein we assume finfluencers are better informed. As a solution, Leland (1979) found that minimum quality constraints or licensing requirements tackle quality uncertainty in high-skill professions (e.g., lawyers or doctors). This can be applied to finfluencers, where typically rather uninformed followers consume conflicted financial (unregulated) advice. However, regulation of finfluencers may also reduce competition in the financial advisory market, which is why regulating finfluencers is not always the best option for consumer welfare depending on the level of consumer harm (Berk and Binsbergen 2022). However, there is currently no literature that empirically assesses the regulation of finfluencers from an economic perspective. While moral hazard provides a useful lens to study commission-driven finfluencer activity, not all creators of financial content on social media operate primarily for personal profit. Many also share content to educate, foster communities, build their personal brand, or even for entertainment (Guan 2023; Mölders et al. 2025). This heterogeneity of finfluencers tempers the reach of the moral hazard argument but still reinforces the need for a quality framework that applies across diverse motivation profiles. FOMO has been identified as a key driver of financial decision-making of Gen Z (FINRA Foundation and CFA Institute 2023), which puts emphasis on how moral hazard stems not only from conflicted incentives on the supply side but also from emotional manipulation on the demand or consumer side. While FOMO is conceptualized as a utility-based reaction to perceived opportunity loss from an economic perspective (Kaddouhah 2024), it can distort rational financial decision-making. Recent empirical studies show that such appeals amplify impulsive, risky and short-term oriented financial decision-making (Friederich et al. 2024; Meyer et al. 2024; Symbiosis and Gandhi 2024; Mölders et al. 2025) and reinforce herding dynamics and overreliance on social heuristics leading to systematic underperformance (Kakhbod et al. 2023; Gerritsen and Regt 2025; Haase et al. 2025). These findings highlight the need to address not only information asymmetries on the supply side, but also the risk of behavioral biases on the consumer side when assessing FinQ. Ultimately, we aim to conceptualize a FinQ framework that helps (corporate) stakeholders navigate the largely unregulated space of financial advice delivered by finfluencers on social media platforms. In markets with information asymmetries, where the true quality of a provider is difficult to assess, such frameworks can reduce uncertainty and support informed decision-making (Stiglitz 2002). Building on this idea, a FinQ framework enabling benchmarking (Cook and Luo 2003; Aiken et al. 2004; Hörner and Lambert 2021) may not only incentivize finfluencers to meet defined quality standards but also foster transparency and comparability. As a result, it might also encourage healthy competition and introduce a form of peer pressure and accountability within the finfluencer market (Calvó-Armengol and Jackson 2010). Quality is described by the international quality management standard ISO 9000:2015 as the “degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of (…) [a service or product] fulfils requirements.” Following this customer-oriented definition, we need to identify quality requirements (or standards) to establish a quality framework for finfluencers. The role of such a “standard setter” can be taken on by different stakeholders including researchers (Soni Raleigh and Foot 2010). In a first step, to measure quality, we must define a set of quality indicators. Therefore, we employ the concept of key performance indicators (KPIs).Footnote 3 Therefore, measuring quality depends on meeting defined KPIs as focal units of the framework.Footnote 4 In a second step, such defined KPIs will be aggregated into quality dimensions (QDs), which will serve as the conceptual cornerstones of the preliminary FinQ framework. This approach is consistent with the grouping of performance metrics into strategic dimensions, as seen in frameworks such as the Balanced Scorecard according to Kaplan and Norton (1992). In addition to considering moral hazard and behavioral biases, FinQ assessment needs to account for an environment of regulatory ambiguity, where jurisdictional inconsistencies and limited enforcement capacity can enable non-compliant or even unlawful practices and emerging forms of financial misconduct (Aggarwal et al. 2022; Miller 2022; Pflücke 2022, 2023; Stefanou 2023; International Organization of Securities Commissions 2025). We acknowledge that compliance under conditions of regulatory ambiguity often operates in a gray zone shaped by evolving standards, limited enforcement, and a strong reliance on self-regulation. These broader structural challenges serve as contextual boundary conditions that reinforce the urgency of establishing transparent and stakeholder-oriented quality criteria. Against this backdrop, we introduce five QDs grounded in the literature that serve as guiding pillars for structuring the FinQ framework: First, compliance with regulation is an inherent prerequisite for quality and gatekeeping dimension for influencer selection, especially in the light of corporate strategic partnerships with finfluencers (Espeute and Preece 2024; Mölders et al. 2025). However, the extent of compliance is dependent on the context, with requirements varying according to the country and the applicable regulation, which often remain fragmented and evolving (Aggarwal et al. 2022; Pflücke 2023). In the development of the FinQ framework, we based our approach on the German and European regulatory framework (see Appendix A). However, such regulatory and legal framework as a basis for compliance requirements must be subject to review and adaptation in each country or market. Consequently, we established QD1 Compliance. Second, we observe that most qualitative KPIs tend to be holistically defined in the existing literature, which is possibly due to their rather limited accessibility and measurability. Thus, we were able to identify rather holistic KPIs such as “post quality” (content quality) and “professional communication” from existing literature (Gräve and Greff 2018; Gräve 2019). However, such KPIs need to be further broken down and refined to be measured accurately. Other connected more specific KPIs refer for example to “playfulness”, which refers to the level of entertainment (Kim and Baek 2022). In response to such holistic content-related and communicative KPIs we formulate QD2 Content and Communication. Third, we also find that “experience” (but also track record) and “expertise” is often associated with influencer quality (e.g., Gräve and Greff 2018; Kim and Baek 2022). This is complemented by KPIs such as “trustworthiness”, “authenticity” or even “attractiveness” of influencers (e.g., Chekima et al. 2020; Primasiwi et al. 2021; Wiedmann and Mettenheim 2021). We refer to attractiveness on a personal level, which has been shown to enhance perceived trustworthiness and expertise through halo effects (Ohanian 1990; Reingen and Kernan 1993). In this context, credibility emerges as a central concept in the literature (e.g., Belanche et al. 2021; Bi and Zhang 2023; Dhun and Dangi 2023). To address the perceived importance of the credibility of finfluencers, we accordingly specify the principal dimension QD3 Credibility. Lastly, influencer marketing practitioners mostly rely on well-established and easily accessible quantitative KPIs such as community size, engagement rate, target group fit, posting frequency, sentiment or impressions (Gräve and Greff 2018; Gräve 2019; Primasiwi et al. 2021). This is also consistent with major influencer audit or due diligence platforms such as HypeAuditor and Upfluence (Alain 2022; Komok 2024). Thus, we derive QD4 Profile Structure and QD5 Engagement. While QD4 assesses how effectively a finfluencer manages their content and cross-platform presence, QD5 focuses on quantitative engagement metrics such as community size and interactions. However, studies by Goldenberg et al. (2024) and Hinz et al. (2011) emphasize that reach alone is insufficient, as true influence depends on content–audience fit, credibility, and network dynamics. This is supported by Lanz et al. (2019) who show that mid-tier influencers can outperform macro-influencers in engagement and trust.Footnote 5 Existing influencer quality frameworks in marketing provide useful foundations for evaluating influencer effectiveness. Leung et al. (2022) highlight influencer selection as a key step, focusing on traits like network size, engagement, authenticity, and brand fit (Gräve and Greff 2018; Lou and Yuan 2019; see also Audrezet et al. 2020). While the FinQ framework reflects general influencer criteria via QD4–5, existing frameworks often overlook finance-specific factors, especially compliance, regulatory risk, and the pitfalls of popularity-driven metrics. FinQ addresses this gap by embedding domain-specific requirements, aligning quality assessment with the unique expectations of financial services. It is built on five core dimensions and over 15 KPIs, which are refined and validated in the following steps (see Table 1; Fig. 1). Conceptual framework Our research is intended to further refine and validate the conceptualized preliminary FinQ framework derived from existing literature. In response to the novelty of the finfluencer phenomenon, we follow an exploratory sequential mixed-method approach according to Creswell and Creswell (2023) for the framework development (see Fig. 2). Therefore, we refined the preliminary FinQ framework through in-depth qualitative analysis of expert interviews, followed by a validation for practical applicability and robustness through a multiple-case study following Dehghani et al. (2022). Research framework Therefore, in the first step, we discussed the preliminary FinQ framework with an expert panel to identify the necessary expertise, partially following the approach proposed by Negrin et al. (2022) and the guidance on qualitative interviewing of Edwards and Holland (2015). Accordingly, an interview guideline (see Appendix B) was derived, which was followed by subsequent pilot interviews (Majid et al. 2017). After a feedback loop, further participants were recruited through online research and direct referrals (Noy 2008). The recruitment process was continued until the point at which theoretical saturation was increasingly evident, as indicated by the level of relevance in Table 2 and the corresponding reduced number of new code categories (quality indicators or KPIs) added sequentially by new interview participants as shown in Table 4 (Hennink et al. 2017). We interviewed various participants, including but not limited to financial journalists, financial service providers, consumer advocates, and finfluencers, capturing multiple perspectives on FinQ to ensure stakeholder triangulation (see column “Stakeholder type” in Table 2; Campbell et al. 2020). The semi-structured expert interviews (59.5 min on average; 12 h in total) were conducted from November 2023 to January 2024. All interviews were held online and recorded using Zoom after verbal and tool-based consent. After recording, the interviews were transcribed using Sonix.ai and subsequently coded and analyzed using the qualitative data analysis software MAXQDA. While fundamentally following the grounded theory of Corbin and Strauss (2015), the focus of this research was to expand the identified quality indicators (KPIs) and refine the preliminary quality framework. In this context, we applied a hybrid deductive–inductive coding approach. The five QDs, derived from literature, were used as top-level thematic categories, while the specific KPIs emerged inductively from the data. Interview transcripts were thematically coded in MAXQDA, with potential quality indicators grouped under the corresponding QD through iterative refinement. Coding was led by one researcher and cross-checked by a second to ensure consistency and interpretive reliability. To further enhance robustness, the built-in generative AI of MAXQDA was used to support the refinement process (Morgan 2023; Zhang et al. 2023). In the subsequent validation, a multiple-case study of individual Instagram accounts (Vaus 2001; Yin 2018) tested the practical applicability of the conceptually derived FinQ framework.Footnote 6 A market benchmark from Zülch et al. (2024) was used to select and benchmark five German-speaking finfluencers with data as of January 2024 (refer to Table 3). The cases were selected based on their market position and popularity, entailing a filtering process (with a minimum of 1,000 followers) and track record (operating on Instagram for at least one year), following the guidance of Miles and Huberman (1994). To ensure variation, cases were selected by gender and topical focus to capture diverse finfluencer types and content strategies. Ten posts per candidate (including pinned and random) were independently assessed in a double-blind review, with discrepancies averaged after a pilot for pretesting and calibration. All expert interviews were conducted in German, and translated quotes are provided throughout the analysis to illustrate key findings (the original German quotations are available upon request). The refinement of the preliminary FinQ framework resulted in a comprehensive set of 40 KPIs (N = 40) within the five previously established QDs. Notably, the most frequently coded KPIs are: (QD1) advertising disclosure and licenses/permission, (QD2) accuracy/correctness, (QD3) expertise/qualification, independence/neutrality and reliability/social proof, (QD4) target group fit and (QD5) community size and reach. In the following, we will use chosen citations of the expert interviews to not only dive deeper into the qualitative data set, but also to support the refined FinQ framework (see Table 4). QD1 Compliance is seen as “(…) the highest priority, it is always the first starting point (…).” (Interviewee 2). Thus, adherence to “Compliance is (…) conditio sine qua non” (Interviewee 4) making it a fundamentally non-negotiable QD with a total of eight KPIs. Consequently, regarding strategic partnerships in the financial services industry, this means managers and practitioners should have “a basic fear, which is reputation at risk” (Interviewee 4). It should be ensured that there is no promotional collaboration with finfluencers who do “not [ensure] one hundred per cent compliance” (Interviewee 4). In response, Compliance therefore follows the “all-or-nothing principle” as gatekeeping dimension. Furthermore, “transparent disclosure of funding” (Interviewee 6) is relevant to avoid conflicts of interest and signal potential moral hazard to followers. In this context, transparency is a high priority regarding advertising disclosure: “if you get money from someone, make it transparent” (Interviewee 7). QD2 Content and Communication encompasses nine exclusively qualitative KPIs, which tends to be a more subjective QD. However, the experts consider “criteria such as variety, ease of understanding and perhaps entertainment value” (Interviewee 3) to be particularly relevant. On the one hand, this dimension primarily concentrates on “the quality of the content, i.e. how high is the quality of the content produced?” (Interviewee 8). On the other hand, the focus is also on “professionalism in communication, (…) [i.e.] how do I communicate with followers, (…) even also if I present myself elsewhere?” (Interviewee 5). Managers and practitioners should therefore pay particular attention to the professionalism of finfluencers when engaging in strategic partnerships. Finally, objectivity is highlighted as an extremely important quality indicator within this QD, even though the business model seems to be conflicted: “One of the key factors is neutrality, incorruptibility [of finfluencers]” (Interviewee 10). QD3 Credibility is seen as another critical QD: “But credibility is, I think, (…) the most important thing.” (Interviewee 10). Following the breakdown and refinement of the KPIs identified in the literature review, a total of nine KPIs were identified for this QD. The experts consider “the personal experience (..) [finfluencers] have [as] very, very important.” (Interviewee 2). In addition, “relevant prior education or self-taught through a track record” (Interviewee 12) are relevant for the experts to assess the credibility of finfluencers. In consequence, “the track record (…) and the (…) expertise [play] a very important role” (Interviewee 8). These KPIs should also be considered by managers and practitioners when screening finfluencers to engage in strategic partnerships. Notably, the experts also stress that “channels where you don’t have a face” (Interviewee 11) or channels “that are based (…) [in a third country] and hide behind masks or pictures” (Interviewee 5) are early warning signs not to engage in strategic partnerships because of reputational risks of potential charlatans. QD4–5 Profile Structure and Engagement mostly cover well-established quantitative KPIs (14 in total), where especially community size and engagement rate are seen as particularly relevant for strategic partnerships: But “We don’t just want to have (…) influencers who have a high reach, we also need good followers who have a high engagement rate” (Interviewee 12). However, some experts hinted not to overestimate QD4 and QD5: (1) as they mostly cover generally applicable influencer KPIs not specific to finfluencers, (2) popular finfluencers “take it all”, (3) dilution of relevance by bots and fake engagement, and (4) there is some overlapping and correlation with the other mostly qualitative QDs (as kind of “residual KPIs” as a consequence of QD1-3). In this context, for a large-scale analysis of high engagement rate outliers and their potential dilution through scams and fakes, especially on Instagram see, e.g., Narassiguin and Sargent (2019). Furthermore, for potential overlapping and correlation of quantitative “traditional” influencer KPIs, see Gräve (2019). We developed a FinQ scorecard based on the refined framework (see Appendix C), revealing diverse quality profiles and content strategies. While some finfluencers combined financial expertise with practical advice, others focused on lifestyle or niche audiences. One candidate was excluded for non-compliance, and another was lacking finance-relevant qualifications. Engagement rates ranged from 0.22% to 1.70%, placing our sample between the 3rd and 40th benchmark percentile, reflecting relatively low levels. This supports prior findings that larger audiences often correlate with weaker engagement, while micro and nano influencers tend to show stronger relative engagement (e.g., Kay et al. 2020; Conde and Casais 2023). Further, the validation demonstrates that the FinQ framework facilitates a differentiation between high-, medium-, and low-quality finfluencers, even though a larger-scale application would be required to establish a robust market benchmark. While individual dimensions (e.g., engagement only) alone provide limited insight, multidimensional integration of qualitative and quantitative indicators reveal distinct quality profiles. Therefore, high-quality finfluencers need to perform consistently well across all QDs, demonstrating strong compliance, expertise and credible communication, while lower-quality finfluencers tend to exhibit selective strengths (e.g. high engagement but weak credibility). This demonstrates the added value of the FinQ framework when it comes to distinguishing reliable strategic partners from those who are less credible, or even potential charlatans. In total, 28 of the original 40 KPIs from the refined FinQ framework were validated and operationalized for the scorecard used in the multiple-case study (see Table 4). To ensure empirical applicability, the selection emphasized practicability and measurability over exhaustive coverage. Specifically, (1) KPIs that were observable and assessable via publicly available social media content (e.g., posting frequency, platform consistency) were prioritized; and (2) KPIs that proved overly subjective, ambiguous, or infeasible for reliable cross-case assessment (e.g., sentiment, self-monitoring, or internal compliance structures) were excluded. Importantly, the removal of 12 KPIs does not imply diminished theoretical relevance; these indicators remain part of the broader conceptual framework but were excluded from scoring due to methodological constraints. A full list of excluded KPIs is provided in Appendix D. To accommodate the varied nature of the KPIs, the FinQ scorecard applies different scale types: binary checks for compliance thresholds, dichotomous ratings for “all-or-nothing” factors like formal qualification, Likert-type ratings for nuanced judgments by analysts, and benchmark-based scoring for quantitative metrics (e.g., engagement rates). This mix ensures rigor while allowing consistent cross-case comparison. We also acknowledge that engagement metrics (QD5) may be distorted by inorganic activity, such as bots or fake accounts, which are known to inflate financial discourse on platforms like Instagram (Narassiguin and Sargent 2019; Kraaijeveld and Smedt 2020). While our scorecard relied on publicly observable engagement data, future applications of the FinQ framework should incorporate bot audit tools and fraud detection to improve the robustness of engagement scoring. The validation demonstrates the FinQ framework’s practical utility and benchmarking potential. It allows companies to identify strategic partners offering a replicable and scalable foundation for finfluencer due diligence in financial services. To enhance practical applicability, we translated the validated dimensions into a concise due diligence checklist (Appendix E). This tool complements the scorecard by offering clear, actionable questions that support consistent, real-world assessments of finfluencers for managerial decision-making. We establish a multidimensional FinQ framework that extends current literature on finfluencers beyond calling for regulation (e.g., Pflücke 2022, 2023). While moral hazard and quality uncertainty persist (AFM 2021b; Arrow 1985; BaFin 2022), conflicted financial advice may still be preferable to none (Chalmers and Reuter 2020; Mölders et al. 2025). Accordingly, the FinQ framework offers a complementary approach to avoid costly over-regulation (Berk and Binsbergen 2022) by enabling stakeholders to distinguish trustworthy finfluencers from charlatans and promote informed consumer decision-making (Stiglitz 2002; Hörner and Lambert 2021). This approach can be reinforced through trust marks, peer pressure, and reward mechanisms that foster professionalization within the finfluencer domain (Cook and Luo 2003; Aiken et al. 2004; Calvó-Armengol and Jackson 2010). At the same time, social media platforms increasingly act as gatekeepers through algorithmic moderation and compliance enforcement (e.g., Schroeder 2025). Their incentive to tackle misleading financial content aligns with broader accountability and regulatory goals, and the FinQ framework could further inform automated “charlatan detection” or other platform-based screening approaches by outlining key dimensions for identifying low-quality or harmful financial content. In consequence, high-quality finfluencers can be powerful allies in promoting better investment behaviors by addressing under-confidence, financial education gaps and risk tolerance (Heo et al. 2021). Notably, they could also contribute to overcoming the barriers that consumers encounter when obtaining financial advice (Moss et al. 2022). This is enabled by audience segmentation based on cognitive styles, enhancing content effectiveness and long-term financial decision-making and literacy (Estelami and Estelami 2024). Ultimately, finfluencers might also impact consumer behavior, supporting the adoption of new technologies through both community-building and endorsements (Nguyen et al. 2024). Our FinQ framework promotes transparency and indicates quality, which is essential for the selection of trustworthy finfluencers for all kinds of strategic partnerships. Thus, our work supports stakeholders to identify relevant high-quality finfluencers with which to engage in partnerships, thereby reducing the risk of reputational damages from finfluencing charlatans (Singh et al. 2020; Hudders et al. 2021). Further, such stakeholders save internal resources and reduce their risk exposure through guiding their selection strategy of relevant finfluencers (Saima and Khan 2021; Ren et al. 2023; Venciute et al. 2023). To further increase managerial usability, we developed two complementary tools based on the validated framework: (1) a FinQ scorecard (see Appendix C) that enables systematic benchmarking (FinQ-Score), and (2) a due diligence checklist (see Appendix E) that translates the validated KPIs into practical binary (yes or no) questions. These tools empower managers to conduct efficient, transparent, and criteria-based finfluencer assessments. In consequence, we see three main stake-holding corporate beneficiaries: First, our work guides sales and marketing managers of financial services businesses to extend their social media engagement and to select relevant high-quality finfluencers as trusted voices and brand ambassadors to especially target the digitally savvy younger generations (Anand and Mishra 2022; Mensah and Amenuvor 2022; Ho and Amin 2023; Chikweche et al. 2024; Lom et al. 2024; Nguyen et al. 2024). Second, our work also supports publicly listed companies in selecting finfluencers for extending their financial communication strategies targeted at attracting younger retail investors to diversify their shareholder basis (Binder-Tietz et al. 2024; Mölders et al. 2025). Finally, our work offers insights to financial planners and advisers, enabling them to gain a deeper understanding of finfluencers as competitors that present a challenge to their current business model (Holger 2020; Tkachenko 2022; Fava 2023; Reiter et al. 2023). In addition to corporate beneficiaries, we find that our research could also support managers of regulatory bodies and policymakers to refine current regulatory practices and consumer protection even though we acknowledge that the FinQ framework can only complement rethinking the current regulation of finfluencers (Aggarwal et al. 2022; Miller 2022; Pflücke 2022, 2023; Guan 2023, 2024; Hall 2023; Stefanou 2023; Espeute and Preece 2024). Our exploratory research is inherently limited due to the dynamic regulatory and technological environment, but also particularly regarding the variable understanding of quality itself. Thus, future research should further differentiate how the FinQ framework handles various finfluencer types, as quality may mean different things for licensed advisors versus unqualified promoters of high-risk assets (potential charlatans). In addition, the FinQ scorecard itself offers the potential to profile such different types (e.g., highly professional, lifestyle-oriented, and entertainment-driven). Future studies could leverage these profiles to map the distribution of finfluencer types across markets and platforms, tracking how they evolve over time. Further, our research is limited by involving an in-depth but small qualitative sample. Also, while mixed-method approaches enhance triangulation of data, they can lead to biases (Campbell et al. 2020; Creswell and Creswell 2023). In consequence, we encourage future studies to employ larger-scale quantitative designs to validate, refine, and potentially generalize our findings, for instance, by testing the FinQ-Score against the actual performance of investment recommendations from a broader set of scored finfluencers. Furthermore, reliance on historical data may not accurately reflect status quo. Thus, further research could aim at keeping objectivity and up-to-dateness (e.g., using AI-powered solutions or follower involvement) and extend the FinQ framework into a longitudinal tool, enabling repeated assessments to capture shifts in reputation and content quality over time. In addition, platform differences present a challenge to applicability and validity of our multiple-case study highlighting the need for cross-platform research. Lastly, we were unable to include all KPIs in the refined FinQ framework due to feasibility constraints, lack of public assessability or observability, and the exclusion of overly subjective or redundant indicators. Moreover, our engagement scoring may not fully capture distortions from bots or fake accounts. Future applications of the FinQ framework should integrate bot-audit tools and fraud detection to enhance robustness. Additionally, our FinQ framework is based on German and European Union law, which might necessitate adjustments of the Compliance dimension for its application to other markets and countries. In response, we call for the development of “generally accepted quality standards for finfluencers” as an extension to our FinQ framework. Finally, future studies could evaluate the FinQ framework’s effectiveness by linking it to actual success metrics of strategic partnerships with finfluencers, such as lead generation, return on investment (ROI), or changes in brand trust. While promising, this step requires longitudinal or other designs that go beyond the scope of our study. Nonetheless, we highlight it as a critical future direction to assess the long-term impact and strategic value of high-quality finfluencer partnerships. Ultimately, other researchers may use our framework as groundwork and provide novel further insights into leveraging high-quality finfluencers as a distinctive opportunity for the financial services industry to cultivate trust, engage with diverse audiences, and develop tailored marketing strategies that resonate with younger consumers in the age of social media. Returning to the introductory quote of the antihero Gordon Gekko, finfluencers currently lack accountability, and their mostly commission-based business model involves moral hazard and behavioral biases which not only causes quality uncertainty, but also potential consumer harm and financial losses. In response, we acknowledge the need for reassessing their current regulation to protect consumers (e.g., through setting minimum quality constraints). However, we see a risk of overregulating finfluencers, which could be not only costly from an economic perspective, but also may take away the opportunity to integrate finfluencers as potential allies in corporate and governmental strategic partnerships. In response, our FinQ framework is a complementary approach to tackle the current lack of accountability. In this context, our findings could accelerate professionalization by enabling informed decision making to eventually drive finfluencing charlatans out of the market. Further, our research builds on improving trustworthiness as a basis for encouraging strategic partnerships with high-quality finfluencers. We also find that regulators and governmental bodies should uphold a direct dialogue with high-quality finfluencers to integrate them into policymaking and strategies on promoting financial education, inclusion and literacy. We find that finfluencers may prove to be more than a short-term phenomenon or a further source of noise within the capital markets. Rather, they may represent a long-term game changer for the digital financial community and the financial services industry. Category Law or rules Requirement Unfair commercial practices § 5a (6) German Act against Unfair Competition (UWG) If finfluencers are compensated in return for posts, referrals, or the use of affiliate links, they must indicate this transparently. § 5 German Telemedia Act (TMG) As finfluencers usually follow commercial intentions because of their business model, they have an imprint obligation. § 22 (1) German Interstate Media Treaty (MStV) Finfluencers must make promotion clearly recognizable and clearly separate it from the other (editorial) content they produce. Financial advertising and capital market law § 34 German Trade Regulations (GewO) License must be acquired from the responsible Chamber of Commerce and Industry (IHK) for any financial advisory activities. § 32 (1) of the German Banking Act (KWG) Advanced licenses need to be acquired for actively trading or managing customer assets or securities. § 20 German Market Abuse Regulation (MMVO) Finfluencers must ensure objectivity and disclose their own interests or conflicts of interest related to financial tips they share. § 12 German Asset Investment Act (VermAnlG) When promoting high-risk financial products, finfluencers may need to disclose a warning in accordance with § 12 VermAnlG. Intellectual property rights German Law on Copyrights and Related Rights) Finfluencers must comply with copyright rules. Data protection German General Data Protection Regulation (DSGVO/GDPR) Finfluencers must comply with data protections rules. Others Private terms of services of social media platforms Finfluencers must fulfill terms of services of corresponding platforms. Topic Question (1) Background and context (a) Please briefly introduce yourself and, if applicable, tell us about your role and experience in dealing with finfluencers. (b) How do you define a finfluencer? (2) Quality indicators (a) What indicators characterize a high-quality finfluencer for you? Optional follow-up question: On the other hand, what indicators do you think characterize a low-quality finfluencer? (b) In your opinion, to what extent do quality indicators of finfluencers differ from those of other influencer niches (e.g., beauty, fashion, fitness, etc.)? (c) Which indicators would you use to assess the quality of finfluencers? (3) Challenges and controversies (a) What challenges and problems do you see in the development of a finfluencer quality rating? Optional follow-up question: Regarding the diversity of financial topics and the different target groups? (b) What role do conflicts of interest and transparency issues play in the quality assessment of finfluencers? (c) Are there ethical or legal aspects that should be taken into consideration when assessing the quality of a finfluencer? (4) Practicability and feasibility (a) What do you think about the feasibility of a finfluencer quality assessment in the real world? Optional follow-up question: Which stakeholders could benefit from it? (b) Which approaches, information sources or methods do you think are most suitable for measuring the quality of finfluencers? (5) Outlook and regulation (a) How do you see the future of finfluencers and their impact on the financial world of tomorrow, especially also in the context of financial communication? (b) What roles do regulation and control play? Optional follow-up questions: What exactly should be regulated and controlled? Who should be responsible for this? Independent sub-score of the dimension Engagement (QD5) [15% Weight] Σ 0.73 0.8 0.8 0.8 0.47 FinQ-Score Weighted quality score Σ 0.8 0.92 0.81 N/A 0.77 Note. F = Filter (all-or-nothing principle); M = Likert-type scale of 0 to 5 (manual analyst assessment); D = Dichotomous scale of 0 or 5 (all-or-nothing principle); B = Likert-type scale of 0 to 5 (peer benchmark). The FinQ-Score is based on five quality dimensions. Compliance acts as a gatekeeper: critical breaches exclude a finfluencer from scoring. The remaining four dimensions are weighed unequally: Content and Communication and Credibility are prioritized as most relevant (35%), while Profile Structure and Engagement are underweighted (15%) due to limited finfluencer specificity, risk of bias from popularity, and susceptibility to fake engagement. Despite a small sample (n = 5), the results show strong quality and diversity, illustrating the practical value of the FinQ framework for future applications. Title: UPCOMING DEADLINE: BlackRock TCP Capital Corp. (TCPC) Securities Fraud Class Action – April 6, 2026 Lead Plaintiff Deadline - Furuqi & Furuqi LLP
URL: https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/03/04/3249533/683/en/UPCOMING-DEADLINE-BlackRock-TCP-Capital-Corp-TCPC-Securities-Fraud-Class-Action-April-6-2026-Lead-Plaintiff-Deadline-Furuqi-Furuqi-LLP.html
Time Published: 2026-03-04T15:50:00Z
Full Content:
March 04, 2026 10:50 ET | Source: Faruqi & Faruqi LLP Faruqi & Faruqi LLP BlackRock Investors With Significant Losses Are Encouraged To Contact Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP Securities Litigation Partner James (Josh) Wilson To Discuss Their Legal Rights If you purchased or acquired securities in BlackRock TCP between November 6, 2024 and January 23, 2026 and would like to discuss your legal rights, call Faruqi & Faruqi partner Josh Wilson directly at 877-247-4292 or 212-983-9330 (Ext. 1310). [You may also click here for additional information] NEW YORK, March 04, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP, a leading national securities law firm, is investigating potential claims against BlackRock TCP Capital Corp. (“BlackRock TCP” or the “Company”) (NASDAQ: TCPC) and reminds investors of the April 6, 2026 deadline to seek the role of lead plaintiff in a federal securities class action that has been filed against the Company. Faruqi & Faruqi is a leading national securities law firm with offices in New York, Pennsylvania, California and Georgia. The firm has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors since its founding in 1995. See www.faruqilaw.com. As detailed below, the complaint alleges that the Company and its executives violated federal securities laws by making false and/or misleading statements and/or failing to disclose that: (1) the Company’s investments were not being timely and/or appropriately valued; (2) the Company’s efforts at portfolio restructuring were not effectively resolving challenged credits or improving the quality of the portfolio; (3) as a result, the Company’s unrealized losses were understated; (4) as a result, the Company’s NAV was overstated; and (5) that, as a result of the foregoing, Defendants’ positive statements about the Company’s business, operations, and prospects were materially misleading and/or lacked a reasonable basis. On February 27, 2025, before the market opened, the Company issued a press release announcing financial results for the fourth quarter and year ended December 31, 2024. The press release disclosed that the Company’s portfolio had significantly weakened during the 2024 fiscal year. Specifically, the press release revealed the number of portfolio companies on non-accrual status had more than doubled, and as a result, debt investments on non-accrual status at cost increased by 289% (from 3.7% to 14.4% of the portfolio). Moreover, the press release revealed that the Company’s net asset value (“NAV”) had fallen 22.44% year over year to $9.23 per share. Total losses, both realized and unrealized, were revealed to have ballooned to $194,895,042 for the fiscal year, a 186% increase year over year, in large part due to a newly added $72.3 million net unrealized loss within the fourth quarter. Despite this, the press release alleged the NAV of the Company was accurate at $9.23 per share, and that “the vast majority of [the Company’s] portfolio continued to perform well,” and the Company was “working closely with [its] borrowers and sponsors to resolve the portfolio issues.” On this news, the Company’s stock price fell $0.90, or 9.64%, to close at $8.44 per share on February 27, 2025, on unusually heavy trading volume. On January 23, 2026, after market hours, BlackRock TCP disclosed certain fourth quarter and full year 2025 financial results, including that the Company’s NAV per share as of December 31, 2025 was in fact in the range of $7.05 to $7.09, 19% less than reported the prior quarter and 23.4% less than reported the prior year. On this news, BlackRock TCP’s stock price fell $0.76, or 12.97%, to close at $5.10 per share on January 26, 2026, on unusually heavy trading volume. The court-appointed lead plaintiff is the investor with the largest financial interest in the relief sought by the class who is adequate and typical of class members who directs and oversees the litigation on behalf of the putative class. Any member of the putative class may move the Court to serve as lead plaintiff through counsel of their choice, or may choose to do nothing and remain an absent class member. Your ability to share in any recovery is not affected by the decision to serve as a lead plaintiff or not. Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP also encourages anyone with information regarding BlackRock TCP’s conduct to contact the firm, including whistleblowers, former employees, shareholders and others. To learn more about the BlackRock TCP class action, go to www.faruqilaw.com/TCPC or call Faruqi & Faruqi partner Josh Wilson directly at 877-247-4292 or 212-983-9330 (Ext. 1310). Title: The AI Jobs Apocalypse Is Starting to Feel Real
URL: http://futurism.com/artificial-intelligence/ai-jobs-apocalypse-feeling-real
Time Published: 2026-03-04T14:02:35Z
Full Content:
By Frank Landymore Published Mar 4, 2026 9:02 AM EST Sign up to see the future, today Can’t-miss innovations from the bleeding edge of science and tech It’s difficult to say how many jobs have been lost to AI, or will be lost in the future. But in tech and financial circles, anxieties over an AI jobs apocalypse are running higher than ever. The other week, it was a viral paper from Citrini Research imagining a dire near future in which vast portions of the workforce are outmoded by AI that had Wall Street quivering in its boots. Weeks before that, Anthropic’s new Claude Cowork AI agent sparked a mass stock selloff over fears that it could automate tasks like legal work, wiping out billions of dollars. Tech leaders have warned of AI’s potential to disrupt the job market for years, but it’s only in recent months that the atmosphere has felt so high-strung. Additional fuel was thrown onto the fire when, last week, Block CEO Jack Dorsey announced that his fintech firm, formerly known as Square, was firing 4,000 employees, or nearly half its workforce. Though Dorsey attributed the layoffs to pandemic overhiring, he also enthused that “intelligence tools” were creating a “new way of working” with smaller teams. This sounded alarm bells across the industry, per a roundup of reactions by The Wall Street Journal. “Square is just the beginning,” former Meta and Salesforce executive Clara Shih wrote on X, responding to a lengthy post claiming that the Block layoffs are “the first AI cut.” “A lot of the jobs that we’ve thrown human beings at the last 20 or 30 years, you won’t need as many human beings doing those same jobs,” Amazon CEO Andy Jassy said in a CNBC interview when asked about Dorsey’s cuts flagged by the WSJ. The fears are warranted, but likely overstated at the present moment. There’s no denying that firms are laying off droves of employees, and there’s also no denying that many of them are openly enthusiastic about AI. One report found that AI was cited in the announcements of more than 54,000 layoffs last year. Those employers include Amazon, which is in the midst of culling 14,000 employees after boasting of the “efficiency gains” from deploying AI across the company. But are autonomous systems actually replacing those jobs? Experts caution that executives may simply be using AI as an excuse to justify cuts that were driven by purely financial logic, a phenomenon that’s being dubbed “AI-washing.” Marcelo P. Lima, founder and managing partner at Heller House, disputed alarmists claims that Dorsey’s cuts at Block were AI driven, calling them the “new Citrini fake narrative.” “Everyone will assume Jack Dorsey… is doing this because of AI,” he wrote on X. “He’s not.” The reality, Lima explained, was that the company was bloated in the first place. Like many tech companies during the pandemic, it had hired thousands of new employees, ballooning its workforce from 3,900 in 2019 to 12,500 in 2022. There’s also little evidence that AI systems are currently capable of taking over human roles in any robust sense. Numerous studies have found that even the most capable AI agents fail at common white collar tasks, while providing little economic benefit: a widely cited MIT study found that 95 percent of companies that integrated AI saw no meaningful increase in revenue. An additional body of research has illustrated how AI can negatively impact human employees, dragging down efficiency and even intensifying work. Still, the fear of something is often more damaging than the thing itself, and if the market is constantly rattled by AI’s still vague potential to disrupt if not upend the job market, it could cause economic uncertainty to fester for years to come. More on AI: Harvard Professor Says AI Users Are Losing Cognitive Abilities I’m a tech and science correspondent for Futurism, where I’m particularly interested in astrophysics, the business and ethics of artificial intelligence and automation, and the environment. By Dan Robitzski By Victor Tangermann By Victor Tangermann By Frank Landymore By Victor Tangermann By Joe Wilkins By Frank Landymore By Victor Tangermann By Frank Landymore By Joe Wilkins By Sharon Adarlo By Victor Tangermann By Joe Wilkins By Frank Landymore By Victor Tangermann By Frank Landymore By Rae Witte By Frank Landymore By Joe Wilkins By Victor Tangermann By Victor Tangermann Disclaimer(s) Articles may contain affiliate links which enable us to share in the revenue of any purchases made. Title: Nvidia invests $4 billion into photonics firms in a bid to bolster data center interconnect supply chains — Lumentum and Coherent investment to fund U.S. R&D and manufacturing facilities, supports capacity rights and future access
URL: https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/nvidia-invests-usd4-billion-into-photonics-firms-in-a-bid-to-bolster-data-center-interconnect-supply-chains-lumentum-and-coherent-investment-to-fund-u-s-r-and-d-and-manufacturing-facilities-supports-capacity-rights-and-future-access
Time Published: 2026-03-04T14:01:18Z
Full Content:
Into the photonic future. When you purchase through links on our site, we may earn an affiliate commission. Here’s how it works. Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox. You are now subscribed Your newsletter sign-up was successful Nvidia has pledged to invest a combined $4 billion in two photonic manufacturing firms, Lumentum and Coherent. As Reuters reports, this continues Nvidia's plans to expend capital investment with its expansive cash reserves to help build the AI ecosystem and improve the output of its models. The quiet part is that it also helps keep Nvidia on the cutting edge. Both companies develop optical and semiconductor technologies, enabling future networking hardware and AI chip designs that utilize light to transfer data, rather than electricity. With Nvidia looking to continue providing the digital shovels in this AI gold rush, leveraging forward-looking technologies like photonics may be one way it maintains its edge in the range of hardware spaces it now occupies in the global AI buildout. “AI has reinvented computing and is driving the largest computing infrastructure buildout in history,” said Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang. “Together with Lumentum, NVIDIA is advancing the world’s most sophisticated silicon photonics to build the next generation of gigawatt-scale AI factories.” Jim Anderson, CEO of Coherent, spoke on their deal, stating: “This strategic relationship underscores Coherent’s role as a key enabler of next-generation AI data center infrastructure. We are proud to expand our 20-year relationship with NVIDIA by increasing their access to include multiple product families to help them build the AI data centers of the future.” Nvidia has been working with both of these companies for some time on its own networking hardware and to develop a strong supply chain for these key components. These new deals only enhance that cooperation. As with most of the deals within and without the AI industry over the last year, Nvidia's investment in these firms is a mixture of hard currency, future purchase orders, and secured access to key technologies. With Lumentum, Nvidia's $2 billion investment will support its R&D and manufacturing expansion in the U.S. with a new fabrication facility. It also includes a "multibillion purchase commitment" from Nvidia, and "future capacity access rights for advanced laser components." The Coherent deal reads almost identically. There, Nvidia and Coherant talk up the "multiyear strategic agreement" between the two companies, which includes "an Nvidia multibillion-dollar purchase commitment and future access and capacity rights for advanced laser and optical networking products." In addition, Nvidia will also invest $2 billion to support R&D and fab. expansion for its U.S.-based manufacturing facilities. Considering the number of bottlenecks that have appeared since the memory supply shortage began in 2025, from glass cloth to PCB drill bits, Nvidia's investment in expanding photonics development and fabrication in America may be a way to get ahead of future supply constraints, too. With the similarities between these two investment vehicles for Nvidia, and the near identical nature of the deals and subsequent press releases, it very much feels like Nvidia is hedging its bets. Arguably, that's what Nvidia has been doing since the advent of this major AI revolution. Nvidia is the one major company making money hand over fist in this new era, and instead of hoarding it, or using it exclusively for stock buybacks, or corporate bonuses, it's been reinvesting in the AI industry that put a rocket up its balance sheets and stock price. It invested in Coreweave, OpenAI, Oracle, Intel, Synopsys, and Nokia, all of which are heavily involved in the AI industry or are adjacent to it. It's keenly aware that even as the king of the AI pile, it needs those other economic and industry pillars to keep the whole house of cards standing. If Nvidia were bold enough, it could try to buy out Lumentum and Coherent entirely. Their company valuations are a fraction of Nvidia's profits from even the past quarter. But it didn't. It secured capacity, but not exclusive rights. Nvidia isn't looking to eat the AI industry and supply chain, but bolster its output. As much as Nvidia is the clear market leader when it comes to AI hardware, it is far from the only player in the game. China has used Nvidia's absence to heavily invest and fuel its domestic inference chip industry. AMD recently signed a $60 billion deal with Meta to power its next-generation AI-powering data centers, and there are small startup companies looking to build out ASIC chips that could prove far more powerful for future AI workloads when fully developed. Marvell's Celestial AI acquisition highlights that other companies see optical technologies as a key future technology for next-generation networking hardware. Meanwhile, the Bill Gates-backed Neurophos is developing AI chips that use photonics to accelerate even beyond what the best GPUs can do. Nvidia made itself the king of the AI hill by having the best hardware at the right time, and as far as training goes, that doesn't seem likely to change any time soon. But if Nvidia doesn't keep developing its hardware, other contenders would emerge. And the competition is ramping up in the inference space, leaving no guarantee that Nvidia will retain its premier position forever. Perhaps getting ahead of the pack when it comes to photonics will be one way it can maintain its lead. But even if it doesn't, $4 billion is a drop in the bucket for a company that made close to 20 times that in the past three months alone. Jon Martindale is a contributing writer for Tom's Hardware. For the past 20 years, he's been writing about PC components, emerging technologies, and the latest software advances. His deep and broad journalistic experience gives him unique insights into the most exciting technology trends of today and tomorrow. Title: AG Investigations and Copycat Anti-ESG Legislation Proliferate Despite Losses in Court
URL: https://blogs.law.columbia.edu/climatechange/2026/03/04/ag-investigations-and-copycat-anti-esg-legislation-proliferate-despite-losses-in-court/
Time Published: 2026-03-04T13:38:31Z
Full Content:
Last week, Texas v. BlackRock (E.D. Tex.), the first antitrust case challenging climate collaborations by financial institutions, reached an initial resolution. Texas Attorney General (“AG”) Ken Paxton announced that one of three institutional-investor defendants, Vanguard, had settled. As part of the settlement, Vanguard pledged not to “direct” its portfolio companies’ business strategies, threaten withdrawal of its holdings unless portfolio companies “agree to act…in some manner,” nominate portfolio directors, or promote shareholder proposals. AG Paxton boasted of having achieved “one of the most significant enforcement actions ever taken against coordinated ESG-driven market manipulation.” Yet these settlement provisions largely duck the question of whether Vanguard’s climate-alliance participation presented meaningful grounds for claiming antitrust harm. On its own, Vanguard’s settlement does little to clarify how ESG or sustainability goals may bring liability for businesses. More broadly, however, this settlement provides a useful moment to step back and assess the impact of state-level initiatives targeting environmental, social, and governance (“ESG”) considerations in asset and corporate management. State legislation seeking to thwart ESG business and investment practices has largely failed in court, as plaintiffs have successfully advanced constitutional challenges. Yet copycat legislation continues to advance in multiple statehouses. And state AGs continue to launch investigations of climate alliances and sustainability initiatives premised on weak antitrust or consumer-protection theories, in an apparent effort to intimidate players most likely to catalyze a clean-energy transition. In American Sustainable Business Council v. Hegar (W.D. Tex.) (“ASBC”), a corporate alliance challenged Texas’s 2021 Senate Bill 13 (“SB 13”), the first law passed by a state that prohibited its entities (including public pension funds) from investing in, or contracting with, firms that “boycott fossil-fuel companies.” As part of SB 13’s implementation, the Texas Comptroller developed a list of 15 firms and more than 300 investment funds purportedly participating in this boycott. Vendors pursuing state contracts over $100,000 were required to verify that they do not “boycott energy companies.” In February, the court granted plaintiffs’ motion for partial summary judgment, enjoining SB 13’s further enforcement. Judge Alan Albright, a Trump appointee, found First Amendment violations because SB 13 was facially overbroad (curtailing an unnecessarily wide range of conduct, including protected forms of speech), and Fourteenth Amendment violations because SB 13 was impermissibly vague (leaving parties unable to reasonably determine the scope of covered conduct). On both counts, Judge Albright cited SB 13’s stated enforcement authority against “any action…intended to penalize, inflict economic harm on, or limit commercial relations with” fossil-fuel producers. The court found that this provision could chill clearly protected activities such as “speaking about the risks posed by fossil fuels, advocating against reliance on fossil fuels, and associating with like-minded organizations.” The court further found that SB 13 already had prompted discriminatory enforcement, because the Comptroller dismissed without explanation some listed firms’ assertions that their purported boycotting actions were “driven by ordinary business purposes” (which SB 13 ostensibly permitted). Texas has filed a notice of appeal with the Fifth Circuit. In perhaps his most far-reaching formulation, Judge Albright declared it “difficult to imagine what sort of conduct is both not an ‘ordinary business purpose’ and not an expression protected by the First Amendment.” Since a boycott not motivated by financial gain typically receives default treatment as protected speech or conduct, and since these bills exempt routine financially motivated decisions (“ordinary business activity” or “reasonable business purpose”) from coverage, precisely what constitutional function does such a law serve? Here, the court implicitly called into question the constitutionality of comparable bills passed by at least five additional states: Alabama’s SB 261 (2023), Arkansas’s HB 1307 (2023), Idaho’s SB 1291 (2024), Kentucky’s SB 205 (2022), and Utah’s SB 97 (2023). Each of these laws, like Texas’s SB 13, appears to be modeled on the American Legislative Exchange Council’s (“ALEC’s”) 2021 draft “Energy Discrimination Elimination Act.” Neither that draft, nor the bills it inspired, explain how they might combat energy boycotts without intrusive intervention into corporations’ business judgment, or infringement of constitutionally protected speech. Additional laws restricting states’ business engagements with ESG-associated firms, and arguably relying on similarly vague or overbroad statutory language, include: Indiana’s HB 1008 (2023), Kansas’s HB 2100 (2023), Louisiana’s HCR 70 (2023), Montana’s HB 228 (2023) (containing facially contradictory definitions of whether ESG considerations categorically amount to “non-pecuniary” decision-making), Tennessee’s SB 955 (2023) (especially vague in prohibiting ESG considerations that “may not be material” to a financial analysis), and West Virginia’s SB 262 (2022). Prior to the ASBC decision, an Oklahoma court in Don Keenan v. Oklahoma had struck down a similar statute targeting ESG business and investment practices, on free-speech and anti-discrimination grounds. But this ruling focused on protections found in Oklahoma’s state constitution, limiting its application nationwide. While the ASBC decision, as a district-court ruling, is not controlling precedent in other districts, its forceful decision on federal constitutional protections offers persuasive authority for challenging comparable “energy boycott” laws outside of Texas. Whereas SB 13 sought to constrain state entities’ investment and procurement decisions, Texas’s SB 2337, signed into law in June of 2025, directly imposes speech requirements on private proxy advisors, based on the particular advice they offer. While this law was partially enjoined by the court within months of its passage, copycat bills have passed in at least eleven other states. According to SB 2337, advisors who factor ESG or sustainability concerns into a proxy recommendation (whether as part of a financial risk-return calculation on an advisee’s behalf, or in pursuit of collateral benefits for a third party) must disclose and explain their weighing of such factors to clients and to the relevant corporation, and must declare that “the advice subordinates the financial interests of shareholders to other objectives.” Advisors who recommend a position inconsistent with corporate management’s must similarly detail the financial analysis supporting this recommendation. Yet recommendations that prioritize religious or libertarian social values, or that conform to corporate preferences, face no such obligations. SB 2337 was challenged by two major proxy advisors, Glass Lewis and Institutional Shareholder Services (“ISS”), who assert that, as applied, SB 2337 violates their First Amendment rights through content/viewpoint discrimination and compulsory non-commercial speech. They also argue that SB 2337 violates their Fourteenth Amendment rights through unconstitutionally vague prohibitions, and is preempted by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 (ERISA) and/or the Investment Advisers Act of 1940. The challenges brought by Glass Lewis and ISS were consolidated in Glass Lewis v. Paxton (W.D. Tex.). In August of 2025, several days before SB 2337’s implementation deadline, Judge Albright, presiding over this case as well as the SB 13 suit discussed above, issued a preliminary injunction that prohibits state enforcement against Glass Lewis or ISS, but does not bar enforcement actions by private plaintiffs, nor state enforcement against other potential defendants. Though Judge Albright has yet to issue a legal explanation for the injunction, his statements at the August oral argument suggest he considers SB 2337 at the very least to violate the First Amendment. Texas filed and then abandoned a Fifth Circuit appeal of the injunction, and a bench trial is anticipated. A coalition of nonprofit organizations has sued as well, in Interfaith Center on Corporate Responsibility v. Paxton (W.D. Tex.) (“Interfaith Center”), arguing that SB 2337 fails on First and Fourteenth Amendment grounds. Their facial challenge would prohibit state enforcement against any organization. These plaintiffs do not solely provide “proxy advisory services,” but argue that SB 2337 defines this term so vaguely (covering, for instance, any party that receives “compensation” for “proxy statement research and analysis”) that the law chills their protected speech. Judge Albright, presiding here also, denied these plaintiffs’ motion to consolidate their complaint with those of Glass Lewis and ISS. Even as these cases proceed, and with SB 2337 enjoined as to the two firms that comprise approximately 98% of the proxy-services market, copycat legislation has proliferated with notable speed, largely based on a model “Proxy Advisor Transparency Act” issued by the advocacy organization Consumers’ Research. In just the first two months of 2026, pending state legislation that would single out proxy advisors who consider ESG factors and/or recommend against management preferences includes: Arizona’s SB 1503, Indiana’s HB 1273, Iowa’s HF 2196, Kansas’s SB 375, Nebraska’s LB 728, Mississippi’s SB 2676, Oklahoma’s HB 4429, South Carolina’s H 4985, West Virginia’s SB 417, and Wisconsin’s SB 879. Kentucky’s SB 183 was also passed last year, over Governor Andy Beshear’s veto. Here again, a determination of SB 2337’s unconstitutionality, via either the Glass Lewis or Interfaith Center challenge, would provide persuasive legal authority against such statutes nationwide. As legal vulnerabilities have emerged for states’ anti-ESG legislation, state AGs have issued threat letters and announced investigations invoking novel readings of traditional consumer-protection and antitrust laws. For example, in January 2025, eleven state AGs (led by AG Paxton) sent a letter to financial institutions, stressing that “radical environmental policies” may violate their legal, contractual, or fiduciary obligations, and inviting responses to help clarify “whether…enforcement action is appropriate.” A 23-state letter (led by Iowa AG Brenna Bird) sent in August 2025 to the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) requested wide-ranging access to internal documents and client communications, while expressing “grave concerns” that this nonprofit emissions-abatement advisor and the financial institutions committed to its standards risk violating antitrust or consumer-protection laws. A five-state letter (led by Florida AG James Uthmeier) sent in October 2025 to the U.S. Plastics Pact, Consumer Goods Forum, and Green Blue Institute cited “concerns about collusion and market manipulation”—and a follow-up 10-state letter notified nearly 80 corporations associated with these plastic initiatives that continued participation may expose them to liability. A sixteen-state letter (led by Montana AG Austin Knudson) also sent in October to Amazon, Google, Meta, and Microsoft expressed “collective concerns” about burgeoning European Union emissions-disclosure regimes, asked “American companies like yours [not] to follow European ESG…mandates…unlawful in America,” and requested a response “explaining in detail the steps…[you have] taken to reject the EU’s anti-American-values CSRD and CSDDD directives.” And a six-state letter (led by Florida AG Uthmeier) sent to Ceres in January 2026 expressed concerns that the climate-transition advocate may have acted “as a central coordinator for efforts designed to…steer capital away from certain fossil-fuel investments.” Equivalent executive-branch coalitions have also arisen at the level of state financial officers, with comptrollers and auditors targeting ESG or sustainability initiatives as suspect “international political agendas.” Individual AG actions have similarly targeted particular firms. AG Uthmeier has issued subpoenas to CDP and SBTi regarding their climate-disclosure services, noting “what appears to be a profit-driven feedback loop” potentially violating consumer-protection or antitrust laws. Since March, AG Uthmeier also has undertaken an investigation and subsequent court filing on whether ESG considerations by Glass Lewis and ISS have misled clients expecting a “sound, apolitical investment strategy.” In September, AG Paxton announced his own investigation of Glass Lewis and ISS for “potentially misleading…recommendations that advance radical political agendas”—underscoring proxy advisors’ current status as an attractive target, as both anti-ESG and pro-corporate voices demand a curtailment of shareholder activism. Not to be outdone, President Trump recently issued an Executive Order directing the Securities and Exchange Commission, the Federal Trade Commission, and the Labor Department to review “foreign-owned” proxy advisors’ conduct for consumer-protection harms, antitrust implications, and/or fiduciary violations. So even as recent judicial developments should give firms and organizations increased confidence that reasonable ESG considerations can prevail in court, blustering accusations and investigations look likely to create headwinds so long as climate skeptics hold executive offices. In the meantime, climate risk for corporations, investors, and society only compounds. Cynthia Hanawalt is the Director of Climate and Business Law at the Sabin Center for Climate Change Law. Andy Fitch is a Climate and Business Law Fellow at the Sabin Center for Climate Change Law at Columbia Law School. Thus, we all are the children of… https://t.co/6HwYMhVlSj — KoGuan Leo (@KoguanLeo) March 4, 2026 Nvidia produces graphics processing units that have become essential infrastructure for AI development and large-scale computing operations. The chipmaker’s hardware underpins much of the processing power required by data centers for training and running advanced machine learning models. The company reported record fiscal 2026 revenue of approximately $216 billion, driven by a 68% jump in data center revenue. Strong AI demand fueled net income growth of 65% to $120 billion and GAAP earnings of $4.9 per share. KoGuan said he believes AI is not a bubble but the start of a transformative era. Title: Why US stock futures suddenly surged today: Dow, S&P 500 and Nasdaq turn green — Is Wall Street returning to its growth path now?
URL: https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/international/us/why-us-stock-futures-suddenly-surged-today-dow-futures-sp-500-and-nasdaq-turn-green-is-wall-street-returning-to-its-growth-path-now/articleshow/129025557.cms
Time Published: 2026-03-04T12:09:27Z
Full Content:
US stock futures turned green early Wednesday. Dow futures jumped to 48,598. S&P 500 futures climbed to 6,837. Nasdaq futures rose to 24,824. The rebound followed a sharp sell-off on Wall Street a day earlier. Markets reacted after reports said Iran quietly approached the US for talks to ease the conflict with Israel. That signal cooled oil fears. Brent crude hovered near $82 while WTI stayed around $75. Lower panic brought buyers back into equities. Investors now watch ADP payroll data and the US jobs report closely. Strong economic signals could push Dow, S&P 500, and Nasdaq further up this week.
